coredata.dc:title: Online adaptive anode potential-controlled fast charging of lithium-ion cells using a validated electrochemical model-based virtual reference electrode

coredata.dc:description: Optimal utilization of the safe operating area of lithium-ion battery cells is fundamental to achieving peak performance. Pushing the currents close to the intrinsic electrochemical cell limits during fast charging of battery electric vehicles reduces charging times noticeably and improves customer satisfaction. With electrochemical model-based state observers embedded in the battery management system, predicting the critical anode potential is feasible. This allows the charging process to be adjusted accordingly for effective prevention of lithium plating. However, validating the model, especially the internal potentials, is particularly challenging, and the real-time capability is often doubted. This work addresses the parameterization and experimental validation of a full-order electrochemical model utilizing cells instrumented with micro-reference electrodes. Special emphasis is put on validating the estimate of the anode surface potential, which enables the model to act as a virtual reference electrode. For the first time, it is shown how this can be efficiently implemented on a next-generation automotive microcontroller, in combination with a charging current controller, for online adaptive anode potential-controlled fast charging. Execution times well below 4 ms prove real-time capability, and reference electrodes validate good agreement between model estimate and measurements in closed-loop charging current control.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Concept of the charging current controller based on the state observer acting as VRE at the anode surface. The upper part shows the experimental setup with multi-RE SLP cells that was exclusively used for validation. In the lower part, the elements implemented on the BMS microcontroller are depicted. A dedicated integrated circuit used to measure the full-cell voltage is shown at the top right.

OCPs of negative and positive electrode determined by GITT in multi-RE cells. Identification of stoichiometric ranges using GITT-OCP data from coin cell measurements and their use for extrapolation outside the full cell (FC) utilization window.

Optimization procedure to determine exchange current densities and solid diffusivity values as a function of the lithiation degree from GITT in multi-RE cells. Sequential parameter variation was performed to minimize the error between the simulated and measured voltage response for each of the 1% SoC steps. Using the spline interpolation shown, the results were implemented in the model.

Simplified illustration showing the vectorized implementation of the calculation of all partial derivatives of the model equations
g
i
with respect to the state variables. Instead of calculating them sequentially for each node, vectorization enables simultaneous processing for all N nodes and thus a significant speedup.

Comparison of PP-p2D model simulations performed with the microcontroller implementation and experimental data. Validation results for full cell (a, b), negative (c, d), and positive (e,f) half-cells, each with the corresponding voltage errors.

Validation of the suitability of the anode surface potential estimate (a, b) to indicate the onset threshold of lithium plating at current rates ranging from 0.5C to 2C. The measured negative half-cell potentials (b, c), DCVAs of the negative half-cell potentials (e, f) and their derivatives (g, h) are shown for the two cell types differing only by the electrolyte used. The results are shown for an arbitrarily selected RE located inside the active area. EIS (i, j) performed during charging at full-cell level is used as a second method to identify the onset of lithium plating. Finally, the current profiles during CV phases can also be used as an indirect method. The vertical dashed lines indicate the point at which lithium plating is predicted/detected by the respective method. Note that EIS was not performed during 2C charging for cell type 2.

Experimental study of the convergence behavior of the state observer upon initialization at different times ①-④during 0.5C CC/CV charging. In addition to the cell voltage (a), the available RE measurements (one RE inside the active area arbitrarily selected) allow direct validation of the model estimate at the location of the RE (b). Furthermore, predicted and measured (by means of Coulomb counting) SoCFC are shown (c).

Demonstration of the closed-loop charging current controller with CC, CAP, and CV phases on the microcontroller for 1C (a), 1.5C (b), and 2C (c). The multi-RE cells enable simultaneous measurement of the negative half-cell voltage, however they are explicitly not used for the controller. Additionally, the resulting current profiles as well as the determined lithium plating onset points (see Fig. 6) are shown. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Performance analysis of the charging current controller implementation with comparison of the execution time for the implementation on an AURIX TC4x microcontroller CPU core as well as optimized for the PPU.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Optimal utilization of the safe operating area of lithium-ion battery cells is fundamental to achieving peak performance. Pushing the currents close to the intrinsic electrochemical cell limits during fast charging of battery electric vehicles reduces charging times noticeably and improves customer satisfaction. With electrochemical model-based state observers embedded in the battery management system, predicting the critical anode potential is feasible. This allows the charging process to be adjusted accordingly for effective prevention of lithium plating. However, validating the model, especially the internal potentials, is particularly challenging, and the real-time capability is often doubted. This work addresses the parameterization and experimental validation of a full-order electrochemical model utilizing cells instrumented with micro-reference electrodes. Special emphasis is put on validating the estimate of the anode surface potential, which enables the model to act as a virtual reference electrode. For the first time, it is shown how this can be efficiently implemented on a next-generation automotive microcontroller, in combination with a charging current controller, for online adaptive anode potential-controlled fast charging. Execution times well below 4 ms prove real-time capability, and reference electrodes validate good agreement between model estimate and measurements in closed-loop charging current control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The software code developed for the AURIX TC499 AA-step microcontroller can be made available on request and for academic use only.

ce:section.ce:para: The battery system of electric vehicles (EVs) is the most expensive component, accounting for up to a third of the vehicle’s total cost [1]. Optimizing battery capacity usage during driving and charging is crucial. In addition to range and total cost of ownership, fast charging capability is a key performance indicator [2] that differentiates original equipment manufacturers from their competitors as it is immediately visible to customers. The battery management system (BMS) incorporates state monitoring and control algorithms required for this and is responsible for the electrical and thermal management of the high-voltage battery pack. It comprises a host microcontroller, sensors for measuring voltages at cell and current at pack level, temperature sensors [3], and often a pressure sensor [4].

Extensive research has focused on developing fast charging strategies that minimize charging time without sacrificing temperature limits and causing accelerated aging [5–8]. Novel approaches aim to push charging current closer to the intrinsic electrochemical limits of cells, moving beyond conventional constant current (CC) and constant voltage (CV) methods [9]. However, the charging capability is limited among other factors by the effect of metallic lithium deposition, commonly known as lithium plating [10]. When the potential at the anode particles’ surface drops below 0 V vs.
Li /
Li
+
[11] due to polarization from charge transfer resistance of the intercalation reaction, lithium plating becomes thermodynamically feasible. This can be facilitated by electrolyte transport [12] or solid diffusion [13] limitation [14]. The reaction is only partly reversible, as the deposited lithium undergoes additional side reactions quickly [12], leading to rapid capacity loss [15] and potential safety hazards [16]. Therefore, controlling the anode surface potential to approach, but always remain above 0 V is an optimal charging strategy with regard to the particular lithium plating side reaction [17], but a particular challenge is that the anode potential is not measurable directly [18]. Instead, laboratory setups can only measure a local potential using a reference electrode (RE) located at a certain distance from the anode surface, otherwise an electrical short circuit to the anode could occur. Previous work using three-electrode setups to directly derive charging current profiles therefore introduced significant errors, particularly at higher currents. These errors resulted in underestimating the anode potential, ultimately leading to an overestimation of the likelihood of lithium plating, as discussed in Refs. [19–21]. However, according to the available information, this has not yet been considered in all recent studies including Refs. [22–27]. Achieving direct current control to a constant anode potential (CAP) can be achieved by implementing appropriate models or data-driven methods [28,29] acting as a virtual reference electrode on the anode’s surface. As an alternative technique, the assumption of constant ohmic resistance was used to correct measured values of reference electrodes to derive the anode surface potential (e.g., Ref. [17]), neglecting electrolyte polarization. Numerous publications utilizing electrochemical models [30–34] and experiments [17,18,20,35] have demonstrated that multi-step CC (MSCC) [36,37] and CC/CAP profiles can significantly reduce charging times and effectively prevent lithium plating. To date, these profiles are typically determined offline [38] and then deployed as lookup tables or voltage-based transition points of an MSCC profile. Due to often unknown initial conditions [8], strongly temperature-dependent kinetics, and the need to adapt to cell aging, it appears desirable to not only store the resulting current profile in the BMS, but to directly implement an online adaptive model-based charging current controller [31,39]. In addition, validating the anode potential estimate is challenging. Experimental model validation has primarily been performed at full-cell level (see e.g., Refs. [30,31,33,40,41]), allowing limited conclusions about half-cell contributions to the overpotentials and the accuracy of the anode potential estimate.

It should be emphasized that in addition to addressing lithium plating influenced by the anode potential in this work in depth, there are further challenges and influencing factors that may be addressed and accounted for when aiming to improve fast charging of lithium-ion cells. A comprehensive overview can be found in the work by Edge et al. [42]. Particle cracking in the anode, but also in the cathode, is a mechanism that may need to be considered as well as temperature effects, among others.

Various approaches for online prediction of anode potential have been explored, including reduced-order electrochemical models [23], physics-based equivalent circuit models [39], polarization-based techniques [38], and data-driven methods [28,29,43]. Zhang et al. [44] developed an equivalent circuit model with separate potentials for the negative and positive electrodes and parameterized it offline using 21700 cells instrumented with a lithium reference electrode. An extended Kalman filter (EKF) was used as a state observer to correct for initial state estimation errors. The results show good agreement between model and experiment for medium to high states of charge (SoC) below 1C. The estimated negative electrode potential was not used to actively control the charging current. Wassiliadis et al. conducted detailed parameterization and validation of a Single Particle Model (SPM) with electrolyte and thermal dynamics using measurements from half-cell coin cell to battery module level [41]. They devised a CC/CAP fast charging strategy with a PID controller that accounts for aging in a simple way by updating cell capacity and solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) resistance [45]. According to the data provided, an implementation on a microcontroller unit (MCU) to evaluate the real-time capability was not performed since the embedded hardware used was a single board computer [45]. A state observer to adapt the model to an unknown state of the cell was not included. Chu et al. [23] used a simplified [46,47] Doyle–Fuller–Newman (DFN) model [48,49], one PID controller as state observer and another as current controller. The results indicated remarkably accurate performance and successful prevention of lithium plating. For currents up to 5C, they performed model validation for the anode potential estimate using a RE integrated into a prismatic 40 Ah cell. However, no temperature model was included despite these high currents. Also, the measured values of the RE were interpreted as anode surface potential without correcting for the inevitable voltage drop in the electrolyte and no model parameters were published [23]. Zou et al. [50] proposed a closed-loop structure for health-aware fast charging using a simplified ordinary differential equation (ODE)-based SPM with electrolyte and thermal dynamics. Following model-to-model validation against full-order models and experimentally for the full cell voltage only, closed-loop charging was demonstrated simulatively on a desktop computer under various constraints including the anode potential. This work, like others known to the authors, lack real-time capable implementations and analyses on embedded target platforms, as concluded by Wassiliadis et al. [8] in their comprehensive review. Performance analyses on computers [28,39,50] with powerful central processing units (CPUs) are of limited value for this purpose and difficult to compare.

From this brief literature review, it is evident that no previous work has developed an online adaptive charging current controller using a virtual reference electrode for the anode potential based on a full-order electrochemical model implemented on a cutting-edge automotive microcontroller. Therefore, this work aims to (i) parameterize a full-order DFN model using single-layer pouch (SLP) cells instrumented with multiple reference electrodes, (ii) experimentally validate the model using these cells, (iii) discuss lithium plating onset, (iv) efficiently implement the model on a next-generation MCU, and (v) demonstrate anode potential-controlled fast charging closed-loop. The experimental validation of the internal local potential model estimates using micro-REs is particularly emphasized. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, such an investigation has not yet been reported in the literature.

It will be shown that using multiple REs and the common SLP cell format proves advantageous for model parameterization and validation of a virtual reference electrode for the anode potential, especially at high current rates, since the measurement results of the individual REs can be plausibilized and inhomogeneities in the cell become observable. Regarding the real-time capability of electrochemical models in state estimators, it will be demonstrated, in comparison to our previous work [51], that MCU architectures that are just about to enter the market push the boundaries further. These will comprise specialized coprocessors that enable parallelization and thus a considerable acceleration of execution along with higher clock frequencies, motivating a reconsideration of previous assessments. The results that will be presented in this work with regard to experimental model validation as well as the implementation an application-oriented automotive MCU and real-time capability assessment of the state observer, comprising a full-order DFN model, can be used in the community for further applications beyond fast charging.

The virtual reference electrode (VRE) at the anode surface relies on a state observer introduced in our previous work [51]. This observer comprises a full-order pseudo-two-dimensional (p2D) electrochemical model with the widely accepted parabolic profile (PP) approximation for solid diffusion [52,53] and an extended Kalman filter (EKF). Implemented on an automotive real-time integrated architecture (AURIX) second generation TC387 microcontroller [54] by Infineon Technologies AG, Germany, it demonstrated significant real-time capability. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, no other work has been published that demonstrates the real-time capability of a comparable state observer implementation. Further details can be found in our original work [51] and Sturm et al. [55]. Nevertheless, key aspects essential to understanding this work are summarized briefly for readers’ convenience.

For both model parameterization and validation, our recently introduced SLP cells [56] are used. These cells are equipped with four gold wire REs (GWREs) of 25 μ m and 50 μ m diameter and a Li4Ti5O12 (LTO)-RE prepared by coating both sides of 20 μ m thick aluminum mesh. The GWREs are located between two layers of glass fiber separator (grade 691, VWR, Germany) inside the active electrode area and the LTO-RE adjacent to the active electrode area, i.e., outside of it. The cells are made of the same LiNi0.8Co0.1Mn0.1O2 (NMC-811) and graphite electrode material as before, with a nominal capacity of 100 mAh. In this work, 1.0 M lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6) and 0.2 M lithium bis(fluorosulfonyl)imide (LiFSI) in ethylene carbonate (EC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and ethyl methyl carbonate (EMC) at a mass ratio of 1:1:1 (Shenzhen Capchem Technology Co., Ltd., China) is used as electrolyte. Additionally, the well-studied electrolyte 1.0 M LiPF6 in EC and EMC at a mass ratio 3:7 with 2wt.% vinylene carbonate (LP57-2, Solvionic, France) served as a reference for model parameterization and comparisons. Additional details on the cells and measurement equipment can be found in our original work.

For application-oriented testing, the cell and MCU were integrated into a custom single-cell BMS. To apply charge and discharge currents, a source meter (Keithley 2460, Tektronix, Inc., USA) was used. The full-cell voltage fed to the EKF was measured with a dedicated automotive-grade integrated circuit (IC) for lithium-ion battery monitoring and balancing (TLE9012DQU, Infineon Technologies AG, Germany). This is a significant difference to prior work that utilized high-precision laboratory equipment [23]. However, the state observer must account for measurement noise, which is realistically achieved by the IC used in this work. The MCU triggers voltage measurements via universal asynchronous receiver–transmitter communication (UART). The data is not stored on the MCU, but transferred in real time via a second UART interface using a custom test automation software to a computer exclusively for data logging. Similarly, via this interface, the charging current controller transmits current values, which are then set by the computer using standard commands for programmable devices (SCPI) in the source meter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model parameterization with multi-reference electrode SLP cells
ce:section.ce:para:
The parameter identification of the DFN model in COMSOL Multiphysics 6.1 follows a commonly used procedure reported in the literature (e.g., Refs. [57,58]). Thus, the following description is limited to special aspects arising from utilizing multi-reference electrode SLP cells. Table A.2 contains the complete parameterization. Given the reasonable assumption of isothermal behavior of the SLP cells clamped between steel fixtures, the model in this work is parameterized at a constant temperature of 25
° C
.

To obtain the open circuit potentials (OCPs) of both electrodes and the electrode balancing, this work applies the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT) directly at SLP cell level instead of the commonly followed fitting approach using half-cell coin cell data (e.g., Ref. [59]). GITT is chosen instead of a slow galvanostatic cycling at, e.g., C/20, according to Chen et al. [57], who compared both methods and observed a higher degree of relaxation and thus more accurate OCP values with GITT. A current rate of C/2 and a pulse duration of 72 s were selected for the GITT, resulting in 1% SoC steps. The rest period between pulses to determine the respective OCP values was 3,600 s, allowing the representation of negative and positive half-cell potentials as a function of full-cell SoCFC without intermediate steps (Fig. 2).

The measured capacity, cyclized lithium inventory, and active material fraction of the electrode volume are used to calculate
Δ
c
s
. Comparison with coin cell GITT data at half-cell level shows acceptable agreement, but not insignificant differences (mean absolute error (MAE) of 3.5 mV). However, the coin cell data are the sole means of characterizing the electrodes beyond the utilization window of the full cell
Δ
c
s,neg
,
Δ
c
s,pos
. This is important because gradients, e.g., during fast cell charging, can locally cause either higher or lower concentrations in the particles or at the surface. Therefore, the coin cell GITT data are used for extrapolation and the SLP cell data from 0 to 100% SoCFC. The coin cell capacity was scaled by area to the SLP cell level and shifted via the characteristic features in the differential voltage analysis (DVA). Assuming that the graphite is nearly fully delithiated at 1.3 V and fully lithiated at 10 mV (LiC6),
c
s,max,neg
≈ 35 , 632
mol
m
− 3
is derived directly from the measured coin cell capacity. Since the structural stability limitations of NMC-811 prevent its full delithiation,
c
s,max,pos
≈ 50 , 060
mol
m
− 3
is obtained from literature values for mass density
ρ
NMC−811
≈ 4 . 870
g
cm
− 3
[60] and theoretical specific capacity
≈ 275 . 5
mAh
g
− 1
[61]. This results in the stoichiometric ranges
θ
0,neg
,
θ
1,neg
,
θ
0,pos
, and
θ
1,pos
plotted in Fig. 2. For the model, only
Δ
c
s
is relevant, not the absolute minimum and maximum numerical values.

The parameterization of electrode kinetics and lithium transport in particles is crucial for an accurate model description, as the model output is very sensitive to this [62]. While the electronic conductivity of the anode matrix is generally reported to be very high (in this work
σ
neg
= 100
S
m
− 1
), the overpotentials caused by the lower and lithiation degree dependent conductivity of the cathode are more significant. In the absence of more suitable parameters, a logarithmic fit was used to parameterize a function according to Amin and Chiang [63]:
(10)
σ
pos
(
c
̄
s
)
= 150 ⋅ 1
0
3 . 802 ⋅
1 −
c
̄
s
c
s,max,pos
− 5 . 4774
S
m
− 1
.
Instead of using a reaction rate constant k and a concentration-dependent correction function with symmetry coefficients [64] to calculate the exchange current density in the Butler–Volmer equation, in this work it is directly determined from GITT measurements as a function of SoCFC. The same procedure is used for the solid diffusion coefficients in the two electrodes [41], whose strong dependence on the lithiation degree has frequently been observed [58] and for which the conventional determination method from GITT measurements at half-cell level without RE can be problematic [65]. The procedure is shown in Fig. 3. From the individual current pulses, the half-cell pulse responses at the location of the physical RE (see Fig. 1) were simulated using the p2D model implementation in COMSOL, accounting for the potential drop in the electrolyte. In addition, this method is not subject to the assumptions made in the classical approach, such as homogeneous lithiation of the electrodes. The process involved minimizing an error function through sequential parameter variation of
i
0
and
D
s
, using the potentials measured with the GWREs shown in Figs. 3c and d. To accomplish this, Bayesian optimization [66] was employed using the bayesopt function in MATLAB 2022b and the LiveLink for MATLAB interface of COMSOL. First, the exchange current density was determined by minimizing the mean square error (MSE) between simulation and measurement about 1.5 s after the start of the current pulse. Next, the MSE for
D
s
was minimized throughout the entire pulse duration, and an offset correction was applied at time
t
pulse
≈ 3
s
to prevent inaccurate initial voltage drops from impacting the optimization result. The results, along with the spline interpolations used in the model, are presented in the lower part of Fig. 3 (e–h). The results for
i
0
are reasonably consistent with available literature data [67] for both electrodes. Note that the rise in
i
0,pos
below 20% SoC is due to the probably non-ideal parameterization of the
σ
pos
function in Eq. (10). The optimization of
D
s
leads to a profile qualitatively similar to previous work [58], despite significant differences in the absolute numerical values. Specifically, graphite displays strongly increased solid diffusivity values in the region of the phase transitions. For
D
s,neg
, the spline interpolation was adjusted for values below
1
0
− 14
m
2
s
− 1
to achieve a stable convergence of the model during constant current (CC) charging, as previously observed [68]. The overall somewhat less robust results for the negative electrode suggest deficiencies in the standard intercalation model and highlight the need to describe the two-phase regions with a more sophisticated phase change model as proposed in the work of Gallagher et al. [69]. However, the implementation of this model is still part of the authors’ ongoing work.

In addition to the electrode properties, electrolyte parameters were determined. Given the lack of a comprehensive parameter set for the dual-salt electrolyte used, multi-RE cells were filled with LP57–2 as reference electrolyte. Parameters of Landesfeind and Gasteiger [70] were used for these cells, with empirical adjustments made for the cells in this study. Measurements of the ionic conductivity showed lower conductivity of LP57-2 [71]. Furthermore, the results imply differences in the thermodynamic factor (TDF). Additionally, a higher transference number
t
+
of LiFSI compared to LiPF6 has been previously reported [72]. The electrolyte parameters used are summarized in Table A.2.

For both the electrodes and the separator domain, tortuosity factors were used to determine effective path lengths within the electrolyte. These factors were applied to adjust the ionic conductivity and diffusivity values. In contrast to prior studies that frequently employed the Bruggeman equation to calculate tortuosity from the electrode porosity, this work utilizes the tortuosity factors measured by Landesfeind et al. [73] for graphite
τ
neg
≈ 6 . 16
and NMC-111
τ
pos
≈ 2 . 73
. These values are significantly higher than those derived from the Bruggeman relation. The Bruggeman equation was utilized to calculate
τ
sep
≈ 1 . 25
for the glass fiber separator due to the lack of more suitable literature values and the high porosity.

To summarize, this section introduced a charging current controller concept based on a model-based VRE with three different operating modes, including the numerical solving procedure. In addition, the experimental approach employing multi-RE SLP cells integrated into a single-cell BMS with MCU was presented, which still serves as a laboratory setup but was realized with application-oriented components. Finally, the SLP cells equipped with reference electrodes could be leveraged for an improved model parameterization approach with a special focus on the individual half-cell potentials.

This work utilizes a further development of the AURIX TC3x platform (Infineon Technologies AG, Germany), previously used to implement the state observer in our earlier work [51]. The AURIX TC499 AA-step microcontroller features six CPU cores with a 32-bit super-scalar TriCore architecture; only the first (CPU 0) is used in this work. The maximum clock frequency is
f
clk
= 400
MHz
, one-third higher compared to AURIX TC3x. It has a static random access memory (SRAM) of
752
KB
per CPU and 24 MB flash memory. The key novelty for its use in BMSs is a special coprocessor called parallel processing unit (PPU) [74]. Based on a Synopsys DesignWare ARC EV processor, the PPU consists of a scalar unit and a vector digital signal processor (DSP). The latter enables single instruction, multiple data (SIMD) calculations, allowing for parallel processing with a vector width of
w
PPU
= 512
bit
. The microcontroller is not yet commercially available, making this paper the first to present detailed performance analyzes and comparisons for implementing an electrochemical model for lithium-ion batteries on this coprocessor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimized vector C code for the PPU
ce:section.ce:para:
Programming of the PPU is done in ISO C extended by special patterns, allowing for the definition of vectors through dedicated data types and addressing the vector closely coupled memory (VCCM). Additionally, extensive library functions for vector mathematical operations are provided. This work implements the complete fast charging controller and virtual reference electrode on the PPU of the AURIX TC499, building on our previously detailed implementation [51]. To utilize the full potential of the PPU, code segments suitable for parallelization were identified in the model implementation. Operations of the same kind that are not directly interdependent in time can generally be parallelized. A basic example is for-loops used to add two arrays/vectors. Vector operations are not allowed in the standard C instruction set, so the result vector is conventionally calculated indexwise, e.g., with a for-loop. However, the vector registers of the PPU enable
w
PPU
/ 32
bit = 16
operations with 32-bit floating-point data in one instruction, reducing execution time by a factor of 16 if
w
PPU
is optimally utilized.

Besides loops, conditional and Boolean statements can be evaluated in a vectorized manner. The PPU features eight predicate registers to store vector comparison results, enabling the masking of specific vector elements during operations. First, this is used to simultaneously search values of SoC-dependent parameters of multiple model nodes stored as splines in lookup tables (LUTs). Second, vector register entries that exceed the discretization size of a domain can be masked, i.e., disregarded.

The software was developed within the AURIX Development Studio (ADS). Synopsys MetaWare, an LLVM-based compiler, was used to compile the PPU code and GCC 11, which comes with ADS, for the TriCore. For both compilers, an optimization level of -O2 was chosen. Due to the different architectures and the underlying compiler frameworks, this may impact the detailed applied optimization passes. However, as this reflects the MCU usage by real users, these differences do not limit the validity of the execution time comparisons in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Model structure adaption of virtual reference electrode
ce:section.ce:para:
Apart from the single loop vectorization, the discretized model (see Section 2.1) offers further significant potential for parallelization. Previously, the partial derivatives of the discretized model equations
g
i
with respect to the state variables (see Eq. (1)) were computed sequentially for each discrete node and inserted into the system Jacobian matrix. Since the equations at the nodes have the same structure and only depend on domain-specific parameters, they can be processed independently and in parallel. Fig. 4 illustrates this. Two limitations of this idealized visualization should be mentioned: If N , the total number of nodes, exceeds 16 (for a 512-bit PPU), a loop is still needed to process the additional nodes. Thus, clock cycles scale approximately with
⌈ N / 16 ⌉
, which should be considered when optimizing discretization. For results in this paper,
n
tot
=
n
neg
+
n
sep
+
n
pos
+ 1
with
n
neg
= 8
,
n
sep
= 2
,
n
pos
= 5
(see Fig. 1) were chosen unless otherwise stated. Calculations involving model equations describing transport processes in the electrodes are still performed sequentially for the negative and positive electrode since the electrode-specific equations and LUTs differ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental model validation at half-cell level
ce:section.ce:para:
To use the state observer with a parameterized p2D model as a virtual reference electrode in the BMS, the MCU implementation of the full-order p2D model with PP approximation must be validated, focusing on the half-cell potentials. This is accomplished using multi-RE SLP cells by comparing the measured
U
RE , i
with the simulated half-cell potentials
U
ˆ
RE
at the same location (see Fig. 1). The results are shown for an arbitrarily selected RE located inside the active area.

Results for various current rates between C/30 and 2C are shown in Fig. 5. Measured currents during CC and, worth noting, CV charging were used as simulation input. The state observer (EKF) was disabled, so no SoC correction was made (open loop). Particularly at the start and end points of charging, and during the graphite phase transition of the fully lithiated LiC12 at 62% (see C/30), the model’s thermodynamics including stoichiometric ranges
Δ
c
s , i
align well with the measurements. Up to and including 1C, simulation and measurements correspond closely, with MAE below 6.5 mV for the cell, less than 3.4 mV for the negative half cell, and not exceeding 7.3 mV for the positive half cell. This indicates well usable results from the optimization procedure introduced in Section 2.3 for the determination of electrode kinetics (
i
0
) and solid diffusivity values. At higher currents, the MAE peaks at 20.8 mV for the positive electrode, 15.7 mV for the negative half cell at 2C, and 13.9 mV for the full cell. The authors attribute increasing deviations primarily to imprecise determination of the model parameters of the electrolyte. At 2C, concentration gradients
Δ
c
l
of more than 3
mol
L
− 1
are reached between the two current collectors at the end of the CC phase, leading to significant polarization.

This work used actually measured [73] MacMullin numbers of similar materials to account for the tortuosities of the porous electrodes (see Section 2.3). These differ significantly from the widely used Bruggeman relation, resulting in higher inhomogeneity in the electrodes. In addition, the influence of the cathode material’s electrical conductivity rises at high current rates. Values strongly dependent on the lithiation degree have already been reported but are probably not described accurately enough by Eq. (10) for the NMC-811 with carbon black used in this work. The comparison in Fig. 5 includes all errors resulting from the discretization as well as the binary32 precision implementation on the microcontroller. Previous work [51,55] suggests these errors are small compared to incomplete model description and inaccurate parameters.

The model that has been validated experimentally at the location
U
ˆ
RE
(see Fig. 1) now estimates the anode surface potential
U
ˆ
neg,surf
(see Eq. (4)), as it cannot be measured directly [18]. When this drops below 0 V vs Li/Li+, the model would predict the onset of lithium plating. The polarization resulting from salt concentration gradients
Δ
c
l
and electrolyte resistance causes the potential drop in the electrolyte
Δ
Φ
l
. Separator tortuosity amplifies this. It needs verification that the zero crossing of
U
ˆ
neg,surf
agrees with the onset of lithium plating.

In addition to the cell primarily used in this work (type 1), results from the cell featuring the reference electrolyte (type 2, LP57-2, see Section 2.2) are discussed and compared. Fig. 6 shows, for seven current rates (0.5C to 2C), the experimental methods used to identify the onset of lithium plating, enabling a direct comparison with the model prediction of the onset point (see vertical dashed lines in Figs. 6a and 6b). RE voltages
U
RE
measured against the negative electrode terminal during CC charging are shown in Figs. 6c and 6d. It should be recalled that these measurements are obtained in the center of the separator domain, which is why pronounced deviations between the measurements
U
RE
and the anode surface potential estimates
U
ˆ
neg,surf
occur with increasing current, as described above, and illustrated in Fig. 1.

The measurements
U
RE
show global minima starting from 1.5C (type 1) and 0.75C (type 2), despite the constant current applied. This phenomenon has already been observed [75] and explained by Chen et al. [76]. They attribute it to the partial transition from regular intercalation reaction to nucleation and growth of plated lithium. This is even more evident from the differential charging voltage analysis (DCVA) displayed in Figs. 6e and 6f and the second derivative (see Figs. 6g and 6h), as proposed by Adam et al. [21]. For DCVA, this work utilizes available
U
RE
measurements instead of cell voltage. Results show a local maximum starting from 1.5C (type 1) and 0.75C (type 2), respectively, before the zero corresponding to the global minimum in
U
RE
. Note that the y-axes are inverted for comparison with previous work.

In this work, this maximum in DCVA is interpreted as the onset of nucleation [76], consistent with existing literature. Lithium metal growth is associated with the global voltage minimum (see Figs. 6c and 6d) [14]. Comparing the two cell types suggests these characteristics are unrelated to phase transitions in the graphite. This feature manifests accordingly as a zero followed by a sudden drop in the second derivative. The rise in anode potentials during plating (see Fig. 6f), leads to a decreasing cell voltage slope [77], ultimately prolonging the CC phase during CC/CV charging.

In addition to DCVA, galvanostatic electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with a 10 mA amplitude was used (Zennium X, Zahner-Elektrik GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) to identify the onset of lithium plating with an alternative method [78,79]. Figs. 6i and 6j, show measurements at constant time intervals in a frequency range between 0.5 Hz and 250 kHz, with only the real part of impedance
ℜ
( Z )
for
f = 5 . 37
Hz
shown (similar frequencies equally suitable). This frequency, near the low-frequency minimum in the Nyquist plot, is suitable for observing changes in charge transfer. However, the characteristic deviation from 1C is only observed for the cell with the reference electrolyte. Notably, this feature appears much later than the one observed in the negative half-cell voltage
U
RE
and its derivatives. No feature is seen at any current rate for cell type 1, yet for 1.5C and 2C this may be due to limited sampling points. Since the impedance change likely stems from additional parallel reaction paths resulting from lithium plating, this change is less pronounced for electrolyte type 1, possibly due to different impedance ratios between intercalation and plating reaction. REs may be more sensitive due to local measurement of the electrolyte potential [56], detecting changes prior to measurable impedance deviations at the full cell level. Hence, the latter seems less suitable for model validation.

Recent research [80] indirectly observed lithium deposition by lithium stripping during CV charging in the current signal (CV phase not shown in Figs. 6a-6j for clarity). This feature appears as a plateau or local maximum for both cell types, starting at 1.5C for type 1 and 1C for type 2. Is is only slightly less sensitive for cell type 2 than DCVA. However, the method only allows an indirect plausibility check and does not identify the onset point. Beyond the scope of this work, the current plateau/increase occurs at similar values for different CC current rates, higher for cell type 1. Based on the detected lithium stripping, it is plausible that the widespread relaxation plateau method [81] cannot detect further lithium stripping in either cell type. All measurements in this work were performed at 25
° C
and reaction kinetics are temperature-dependent. Therefore, the results at colder temperatures, where the plateau method is commonly used, may deviate from those reported here [82,83].

In summary, for comparing the model predictions with measurement results, DCVA of the negative half-cell voltage
U
RE
is considered the most sensitive and thus conservative method for determining the onset of lithium plating for model predictive control. The zero crossing of the model estimate of the anode surface potential
U
ˆ
neg,surf
aligns well with the onset of lithium plating for both electrolytes used. The estimate drops below 0 V vs. Li/Li+
< 1
mAh
ahead of the onset point determined experimentally for cell type 1 and
< 3 . 5
mAh
ahead for cell type 2. This can be derived by comparing the vertical dashed lines in Figs. 6a and 6b with the respective lines in Figs. 6c and 6d.

The virtual reference electrode at the anode surface, validated by
U
RE
measurements and the lithium plating onset point, incorporates an EKF (see Section 2.1). This was validated in our previous work only by noise-corrupted simulations with identical parameterization and is now experimentally tested using the custom single-cell BMS (Section 2.2). The state observer running on the microcontroller is deployed in Observe mode, as shown in Fig. 1. Furthermore, the readings from the REs are used to verify cell voltage accuracy, specifically at half-cell level. The chosen update rate is
≈ 2
Hz
, balancing state observer convergence and MCU resource utilization. Although current BEVs use higher update rates (10–100 Hz), ongoing research seeks to reduce per-cell update rates for scalability to multiple cells in battery packs. For scalability reasons, an update rate significantly below that currently used in BEVs was chosen in this work, which may even have the potential to be reduced further depending on the dynamics of the applied current or the maximum current rate during active current control. It is suspected that the minimum required update rate to ensure a robust convergence of the state observer also depends on the accuracy of the model parameterization. A systematic investigation of the lower bound was not performed and could be an interesting future research question.

The state observer’s stability is best analyzed during initialization, as the model must adapt to the cell’s unknown SoC. As a result, the largest errors
y
( t )
between predicted
U
ˆ
cell
( t )
and measured cell voltage
U
cell
( t )
occur (see Fig. 1). Fig. 7 presents four initialization times during an intentionally for 15 min interrupted 0.5C CC/CV charging profile. The interruption allows to verify the convergence stability during relaxation and without applied current. At time ①, after exceeding 3.0 V, the state observer promptly identifies the correct SoC, as seen in Fig. 7c, yielding correct estimates of the negative half-cell voltage due to the steep OCP of graphite at this lithiation degree. In addition, the initialization occurred shortly after the start of charging, which is why the electrodes are relatively homogeneous and electrolyte concentration gradients are low. This is not the case for initialization point ② (3.7 V). It may seem from the cell voltage that
U
ˆ
cell
identifies the correct state directly. However, Fig. 7c, reveals that the SoC determined via Coulomb counting shows that the
SoC
FC
is overestimated by more than ten percentage points. This is due to the initialization routine where the state observer assumes open circuit conditions (see Ref. [55] for details). Consequently, the error increases initially over the next few minutes before the observer converges to the correct state. This takes longest for the SoC with
≈ 15
min, while the negative half-cell potential is identified within
≈ 11
min with a deviation of less than 2 mV. This is because SoC errors do not have a strong impact on the anode potential due to its relatively flat OCP in this region. The
SoC
FC
of the state observer shown in Fig. 7c was directly determined from the positive electrode average concentration. At ③the observer is activated at the end of the relaxation phase. The accurate estimation results observed confirm the hypothesis that initialization at zero current facilitates fast recovery of all states. At ④, a behavior similar to ②is observed.

The implemented EKF can be robustly initialized at different cell states, even with real measurement data and realistic noise, using an automotive-grade IC for cell voltage measurement (see Section 2.2). In the following, a relevant EKF parameter is briefly discussed for the readers’ convenience and reproducibility of the results. The measurement noise covariance r was set to
5 ⋅ 1
0
− 4
V
2
, five times higher than in our previous work [51]. Both values exceed the actual measurement noise of the utilized sensor (
< 1
0
− 6
V
2
). The lower r , the closer the EKF follows the measured voltage, and the less it trusts the model prediction. Assuming a perfectly parameterized model, this may be reasonable, but the non-negligible inaccuracies of the PP-p2D model in this work (see Fig. 5) would lead to incorrect estimates of the SoC and, consequently, the internal states. Hence, errors in one parameter of the positive electrode could also affect the negative half-cell voltage estimate, for instance. Therefore, after extensive investigation, it has been found appropriate to adjust the measurement noise covariance r to allow cell voltage errors y within the range of those identified in the open-loop model validation. Nonetheless, robust initialization and fast recovery of all states is still achieved, as demonstrated in Fig. 7.

After validating the electrochemical model-based virtual reference electrode, its state variables can be applied to actively control the charging current in real time using the developed microcontroller implementation. For this purpose, the Control mode (see Fig. 1) is activated upon successful initialization of the state observer as described in the previous section, allowing for the assumption of valid values for the state variables. A three-stage charging procedure is run, consisting of a CC, CAP, and CV phase. Starting at 2.9 V, a constant current is applied until a threshold value of the estimated anode surface potential is reached. Thereupon, the CAP-based charging phase begins by considering expression (c) in Fig. 1. Once the upper voltage limit of 4.2 V is reached, the controller switches automatically to expression (b) in Fig. 1, i.e., CV charging. During this phase, the applied current is actively controlled by the microcontroller and not by a voltage limit of the source meter. Assuming perfect parameterization of the model, the target minimum anode potential
U
neg,surf,min
could be set to 0 V, as shown by the identification of the lithium plating onset points in Section 4.2. However, there are several reasons to add a safety margin: First, experimental model validation revealed inaccuracies, especially at high current rates (see Section 4.1), which also affects the anode surface potential. Second, anode potential inhomogeneity is known to occur especially in larger-format cells and at the edges of the active electrode area. Therefore,
U
neg,surf,min
= 20
mV
is chosen for the controller in agreement with previous work [23,35,84,85].

Fig. 8 demonstrates the function of the current controller at three current rates (1C, 1.5C, and 2C) in the CC phase. The estimated anode surface potential
U
ˆ
neg,surf
(in green), the negative half-cell voltage
U
ˆ
RE
(in blue), and
U
RE , i
measured by the REs of the multi-RE SLP cells. The latter were measured simultaneously but have no connection to the controller, unlike Ref. [35], who actively controlled for the measured value of a RE. The shaded area represents the spread between the GWREs (
i = 1 , … , 4
) within the active electrode area (see Section 2.2), and the solid black line represents the mean. The cell voltage estimate
U
ˆ
cell
and corresponding measurement
U
cell
with the IC connected via UART are also shown. Finally, Fig. 8d summarizes the resulting current profiles.

Comparing different initial current rates demonstrates the successful implementation and functionality of the charging controller across all three settings. As current increases, the CC phase shortens, reaching the target minimum value of the anode potential earlier. The voltage drop between the RE location and anode surface increases. Consequently, the CAP phase starts at lower capacities, while the transition to the CV phase remains constant. At 1C, there is a good agreement between estimated and measured cell voltage, as well as negative half-cell voltage. As a result, a stable anode potential control to
U
neg,surf,min
is achieved with less than
± 1
mV
deviation. This outcome is also observed for the cell voltage in the CV phase. At 1.5C as shown in Fig. 8b, which would lead to lithium plating as a full CC/CV charging protocol already according to the analysis in Section 4.2, the controller continues to perform reliably. However, the deviations between estimated and measured cell voltage are slightly larger, likely due to the already discussed inaccuracies in the model parameterization. At 2C, deviations become more pronounced and show up already in the CC phase. This is mainly due to the EKFs incorrect determination of the SoC resulting from errors in the cell voltage estimation (see Fig. 5b). This impacts the negative electrode voltage, especially at low lithiation degrees. This leads to interference between the state observer and the current controller during the CAP phase, causing deviations of up to
± 8
mV
vs.
U
neg,surf,min
. Comparing the estimated voltage of
U
ˆ
RE
with RE measurements reveals potential for model optimization at such a very high current rate for this particular cell.

The current profiles resulting from the controller shown in Fig. 8d follow a complex trajectory. The applied current was matched with the state observer update rate of
≈ 2
Hz
. The results obtained are partly due to the non-linear curves for solid diffusivity and exchange current density (see Fig. 3) determined by the introduced optimization method. In addition, the model discretization, specifically of the negative electrode, had minimal influence. To avoid increasing currents when
U
ˆ
RE
>
U
neg,surf,min
, only calculated current values for
U
ˆ
RE
≤
U
neg,surf,min
were set in the source meter. This explains the steps and constant current phases, such as those observed for 2C between roughly 58 mAh and 72 mAh. Without this implementation, the current would increase sharply in this range to lower the overly high anode surface potential to 20 mV. However, this would cause the full-cell voltage to rise, increasing the existing cell voltage error and potentially destabilize the system. Thus, this effect is also a challenge of the partially not optimal model description. Both 1.5C and 2C current profiles fall within the previously determined safe operating area of the cell, as evidenced by the plotted markers. At 2C, part of the 20 mV safety margin is consumed by the slightly less accurate control, demonstrated by the small distance of the lithium plating onset point at 1.75C. However, for 1.5C, it may be feasible to set
U
neg,surf,min
= 0
V
without exceeding the safe operating area limits.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time capability of the full controller
ce:section.ce:para:
So far, the performance of the charging current controller using the virtual reference electrode has been analyzed in terms of agreement with the validation data. Finally, the real-time capability of the optimized microcontroller implementation (detailed in Section 3) is briefly analyzed – a crucial aspect for assessing the method’s applicability in BMSs of EVs. Our previously presented implementation [51], extended by the full charging current controller, was first ported to a CPU core of the AURIX TC499 microcontroller. Before that, the parameterization was adapted as per Table A.2 and detailed in Section 2.3. Notably, the new parameterization of electrode kinetics and lithium transport and a slightly increased discretization from 6-2-6 to 8-2-5, resulted in increased model complexity. One reason for this is that an increased discretization leads to a higher number of nodes at which the model equations have to be evaluated and the total size of the equation system that is solved via a Newton–Raphson approach increases, as described in Section 2.1. The execution time for a step size of
≈ 0 . 5
s
is evaluated in Table 1.

The next step involved implementing the code without changes on the dedicated coprocessor (PPU). Using the common compiler optimization level -O2, this resulted in a decreased execution time of the JacobianCalc part, but a 3% increase in the charging current controller (CCC) execution time because of less efficient matrix operations. Given the significantly larger word width of
w
PPU
= 512
bit
compared to
w
TriCore
= 32
bit
, this result is by no means satisfactory. Therefore, model structure adaptation (see Fig. 4) and implementation of additional code optimization steps described in Section 3.3 were performed. The extensive manual work resulted in the Vector C implementation, whose execution times are presented in Table 1. In the future, an automated toolchain will help to facilitate this. Remarkably, the execution time is reduced by 90.1% for the CCC including VRE. The impact is particularly large in the code section labeled JacobianCalc, which greatly benefits from the adapted model structure. The EKF also gains a significant speedup as the nested for-loops required for the matrix operations could be vectorized. In contrast, the speedup for solving the linear equation system (LES) using lower–upper (LU) decomposition in SolveBandLES, which has already been optimized in our earlier work, is limited due to the limited parallelization capabilities. For the charging procedure used in the analysis (see Fig. 8b), the average iteration count is
n
̄
iter
= 2 . 9
(including CCC) and does not exceed 4. To compute a consistent solution after the EKF update, additional
n
̄
iter,EKF
= 1 . 23
iterations are required, initially at most 3.

In summary, the development of a model implementation tailored to the architecture of the novel coprocessor significantly enhances the applicability of electrochemical models for state estimation and control in BMSs. The execution time of 3.41 ms (for the state observer implementation presented in Ref. [51] even 2.08 ms was reached) enables scaling from cell level to battery pack, while still achieving update rates of 1–5 Hz. This is valuable for applications like charging current control and more.

Optimizing the charging process of battery electric vehicles through online adaptive control close to the intrinsic electrochemical limits of lithium-ion battery cells is a research goal with significant relevance to the industry. This work proposes an anode potential-controlled fast charging current controller using a validated electrochemical model-based virtual reference electrode implemented with high real-time capability on a next-generation automotive microcontroller. It bridges the gap between complex offline simulation frameworks and closed-loop online application in battery management systems. Using multi-reference electrode single-layer pouch (SLP) cells, parameterization, validation, and testing of the virtual reference electrode and controller are performed with measurements of internal local potentials up to 2C. Key findings include:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Model parameterization and validation are facilitated using cells instrumented with micro-reference electrodes and further improved by the ability to assign processes to the individual electrodes, especially at higher current rates.
The state observer can be robustly initialized, and the charging current controller is verified to function up to 2C, while its accuracy is highly dependent on the fidelity of the underlying model.

A total execution time of 3.4 ms (2.1 ms for the p2D model) per time step could be reached on a dedicated coprocessor. The acceleration by a factor of more than 10 shows how an optimized implementation tailored to the special architecture further pushes the limits of real-time capability of electrochemical models.

ce:section.ce:para: Challenges identified for further research include the obvious dependence of the accuracy of the results on the underlying model description and parameterization, which was discussed repeatedly in this work, indicating that future work needs to improve this. More elaborated phase change models [69] and extensive experimental work to determine the electrolyte parameters [70] for the multitude of different solvents and salts are just two examples. In addition, temperature effects and inhomogeneity in larger format cells as well as other potentially occurring side reactions must inevitably be considered in this regard. The authors are currently and prospectively investigating methods to identify and adapt the model parameters to the aging state of the cell, ensuring stable, high accuracy throughout the cell’s lifetime for optimal state estimation and control algorithms. Beyond this, the proposed concept offers opportunities to develop and implement additional algorithms, e.g., for power and energy prediction.
